g.data-narration: Welcome to Virtual Memory and File Systems - two fundamental concepts in modern operating systems.
Virtual memory creates an illusion of larger memory by using disk space as an extension of RAM, enabling process isolation and memory protection.
File systems organize data hierarchically, providing infrastructure for storing, retrieving, and managing files on secondary storage devices.
The MMU translates virtual addresses to physical addresses using page tables. When a page is not in memory, a page fault triggers the OS to load it from disk.
Demand paging loads pages only when needed, saving memory and I/O operations. The working set model keeps actively used pages in memory.
Page replacement algorithms select victim pages when memory is full. Key algorithms include FIFO, OPT, LRU, and Clock - each with different trade-offs.
FIFO removes the oldest page in memory. With 3 frames and reference string 7,0,1,2,0,3,0,4,2,3,0,3,2 - we get 9 page faults.
LRU evicts the least recently used page, approximating optimal replacement but requiring hardware support. Clock algorithm uses reference bits for efficiency.
g.text: LRU: Better than FIFO, approximates OPT

g.data-narration: Segmentation divides memory into logical segments like code, data, stack, and heap, providing natural protection boundaries between program components.
File systems use inodes to store metadata and block pointers. Directories map file names to inode numbers for name-based access.
g.text: Each lookup reads directory, finds inode number
Cached in dentry cache for performance

g.data-narration: Contiguous allocation offers fast access but causes fragmentation. Linked allocation eliminates fragmentation but limits random access. Indexed allocation provides both.
